quiz_questions.question_text: Enthalpy (H) measures:

options.text: Temperature of the surroundings
Heat content of a system at constant pressure
Free energy of the system
Disorder of a system

quiz_questions.explanation: Enthalpy (H) measures the heat content of a system at constant pressure. Changes in enthalpy (DeltaH) indicate heat flow.

quiz_questions.question_text: A negative DeltaH means the reaction is:

options.text: Endothermic (absorbs heat)
At equilibrium currently
Exothermic (releases heat)
Impossible to occur

quiz_questions.explanation: A negative DeltaH means the system lost heat (released it to the surroundings), so the reaction is exothermic.

quiz_questions.question_text: Entropy (S) measures:

options.text: Temperature of the system
Heat content of the system
The disorder or randomness of a system
Free energy of the system

quiz_questions.explanation: Entropy (S) measures the disorder or randomness of a system. More disorder = higher entropy.

quiz_questions.question_text: The second law of thermodynamics states:

options.text: Heat flows from cold to hot
Entropy always decreases
Entropy of the universe tends to increase
Energy is always conserved

quiz_questions.explanation: The second law states that the total entropy of the universe tends to increase. Natural processes favor greater disorder.

quiz_questions.question_text: Gibbs free energy (G) determines:

options.text: The pressure of the system only
The temperature of the system only
The color of the products formed
Whether a reaction is spontaneous

quiz_questions.explanation: Gibbs free energy (DeltaG) determines spontaneity. If DeltaG < 0, the reaction is spontaneous.

quiz_questions.question_text: A reaction is spontaneous when DeltaG is:

options.text: Infinite in value
Exactly zero
Negative
Positive

quiz_questions.explanation: A reaction is spontaneous when DeltaG < 0. This means the reaction can proceed without continuous outside energy input.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Gibbs free energy equation is:

options.text: DeltaG = DeltaH + TDeltaS
DeltaG = DeltaH / TDeltaS
DeltaG = DeltaH - TDeltaS
DeltaG = DeltaH x DeltaS

quiz_questions.explanation: DeltaG = DeltaH - TDeltaS, where T is temperature in Kelvin, DeltaH is enthalpy change, and DeltaS is entropy change.

quiz_questions.question_text: A positive DeltaH means the reaction is:

options.text: Exothermic (releases heat)
At equilibrium currently
Endothermic (absorbs heat)
Spontaneous at all temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: A positive DeltaH means the system gained heat from the surroundings, so the reaction is endothermic.

quiz_questions.question_text: Entropy increases when:

options.text: A gas condenses into a liquid
A solid forms from a liquid
A liquid evaporates into a gas
Particles slow down and stop

quiz_questions.explanation: Entropy increases when substances go from more ordered to less ordered states: solid -> liquid -> gas. Evaporation increases entropy.

quiz_questions.question_text: When DeltaG = 0, the system is at:

options.text: Absolute zero temperature
Maximum disorder state
Equilibrium
Maximum spontaneity

quiz_questions.explanation: When DeltaG = 0, the system is at equilibrium. The forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is enthalpy (DeltaH)?

options.text: The measure of heat change in a reaction at constant pressure
The measure of disorder or randomness in a chemical system
The energy available to do useful work in a chemical system
The temperature at which a reaction becomes spontaneous

quiz_questions.explanation: Enthalpy (DeltaH) is the heat change at constant pressure. Negative DeltaH = exothermic; positive DeltaH = endothermic.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is entropy (DeltaS)?

options.text: A measure of disorder or randomness in a system or its surroundings
A measure of the total heat content of a system at constant pressure
The energy available to do useful work in a chemical reaction change
The rate at which a chemical reaction reaches equilibrium in solution

quiz_questions.explanation: Entropy (S) is a measure of disorder or randomness. DeltaS is the change in entropy during a process. Positive DeltaS means increased disorder.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Gibbs free energy (DeltaG)?

options.text: The energy available to do useful work, combining enthalpy and entropy effects
The total kinetic energy of all particles in a system at a given temperature
The energy needed to break all chemical bonds in the reactant molecules
The heat absorbed or released during a phase change at constant pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: Gibbs free energy combines enthalpy and entropy: DeltaG = DeltaH - TDeltaS. It predicts spontaneity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define 'spontaneous reaction' in thermodynamics.

options.text: A reaction that proceeds without continuous outside energy input (DeltaG < 0)
A reaction that occurs instantly without any activation energy being required
A reaction that always releases heat to the surrounding environment nearby
A reaction that only occurs at very high temperatures above 1,000 degrees C

quiz_questions.explanation: A spontaneous reaction has DeltaG < 0 and can proceed on its own. Note: spontaneous does not mean fast -- it means thermodynamically favorable.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'first law of thermodynamics'?

options.text: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred or converted in form
The entropy of the universe always increases during any natural process
At absolute zero, the entropy of a perfect crystal is exactly equal to zero
Heat always flows spontaneously from cold objects to hotter objects nearby

quiz_questions.explanation: The first law of thermodynamics (conservation of energy) states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred or converted.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'exothermic' mean in terms of enthalpy?

options.text: DeltaH is negative because the system releases heat to the surroundings
DeltaH is positive because the system absorbs heat from the surroundings
DeltaH is zero because no heat is exchanged between system and surroundings
DeltaH is undefined because exothermic reactions do not have enthalpy values

quiz_questions.explanation: Exothermic means DeltaH < 0. The system releases heat, so the products have less energy than the reactants.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the 'second law of thermodynamics' state?

options.text: The total entropy of an isolated system always tends to increase over time
Energy cannot be created or destroyed in any chemical or physical process
The entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero is exactly equal to zero
All spontaneous reactions must be exothermic and release heat to surroundings

quiz_questions.explanation: The second law states that the total entropy of an isolated system always tends to increase. Natural processes favor greater disorder.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'nonspontaneous' in thermodynamics?

options.text: A process that requires continuous energy input to proceed (DeltaG > 0)
A process that occurs very slowly but will eventually complete on its own
A process that releases heat but does not produce any useful work output
A process that only occurs at extremely low temperatures near absolute zero

quiz_questions.explanation: A nonspontaneous process has DeltaG > 0 and requires continuous energy input to occur. The reverse of a nonspontaneous process is spontaneous.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'absolute zero' in thermodynamics?

options.text: 0 Kelvin (-273.15 degrees C), the lowest possible temperature
0 degrees Celsius, the freezing point of pure water at standard pressure
0 degrees Fahrenheit, the temperature of an ice and salt water mixture
The temperature at which all chemical reactions stop occurring completely

quiz_questions.explanation: Absolute zero is 0 K (-273.15 degrees C), the theoretical lowest temperature where molecular motion approaches its minimum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the variable T represent in DeltaG = DeltaH - TDeltaS?

options.text: Temperature in Kelvin, which must always be a positive value
Temperature in Celsius, which can be positive or negative
Time in seconds for the reaction to reach equilibrium
The number of moles of product formed during the reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: T is the absolute temperature in Kelvin. It must be in Kelvin (not Celsius) for the Gibbs equation to work correctly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student finds that a reaction has DeltaH = -100 kJ and DeltaS = +50 J/K. At 298 K, the reaction is:

options.text: Always spontaneous because both DeltaH is negative and DeltaS is positive
Never spontaneous because the enthalpy is negative in this reaction
Spontaneous only at very low temperatures below the freezing point
At equilibrium because DeltaG equals zero at this temperature value

quiz_questions.explanation: With negative DeltaH and positive DeltaS, both terms favor spontaneity: DeltaG = DeltaH - TDeltaS will always be negative at any temperature.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ice melting at room temperature is spontaneous even though it absorbs heat. This is because:

options.text: The large increase in entropy (DeltaS > 0) makes DeltaG negative overall
Melting does not actually absorb any heat from the surrounding environment
The enthalpy change is negative because melting releases heat to the air
The temperature has no effect on whether the melting process is spontaneous

quiz_questions.explanation: Melting ice is endothermic (DeltaH > 0) but has a large positive DeltaS. At room temperature, the TDeltaS term is large enough to make DeltaG negative.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is told that a reaction has DeltaH = +200 kJ and DeltaS = -100 J/K. This reaction is:

options.text: Never spontaneous at any temperature because both terms disfavor spontaneity
Always spontaneous at any temperature because of the large enthalpy value
Spontaneous only at high temperatures where the TDeltaS term dominates
Spontaneous only at low temperatures where the DeltaH term dominates it

quiz_questions.explanation: Positive DeltaH and negative DeltaS both make DeltaG more positive: DeltaG = (+) - T(-) = (+) + (+) = always positive. Never spontaneous.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chemist heats water until it boils. At exactly 100 degrees C and 1 atm, the liquid-gas transition has DeltaG equal to:

options.text: Zero because the system is at equilibrium at the boiling point
A large negative number because boiling releases energy to air
A large positive number because boiling requires energy input
An undefined value because phase changes have no free energy

quiz_questions.explanation: At a phase transition temperature (boiling/melting point at 1 atm), the system is at equilibrium, so DeltaG = 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: A biologist explains that living cells maintain low entropy internally. This does not violate the second law because:

options.text: Cells increase the entropy of their surroundings by more than they decrease their own
Living organisms are exempt from the laws of thermodynamics that apply to chemicals
Entropy only applies to gases and not to biological systems in living organisms
The second law only applies to closed systems and cells are not closed systems

quiz_questions.explanation: The second law applies to the universe (system + surroundings). Cells decrease their own entropy but increase the entropy of surroundings even more, so total entropy increases.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student calculates DeltaG for a reaction: DeltaH = -50 kJ, DeltaS = -100 J/K, T = 300 K. The DeltaG value is:

options.text: -20 kJ, so the reaction is spontaneous at this temperature
+20 kJ, so the reaction is nonspontaneous at this temperature
0 kJ, so the reaction is at equilibrium at this temperature
-80 kJ, so the reaction is very strongly spontaneous at this temp

quiz_questions.explanation: DeltaG = DeltaH - TDeltaS = -50 kJ - (300 K)(-0.100 kJ/K) = -50 + 30 = -20 kJ. (Convert J to kJ: -100 J/K = -0.100 kJ/K.)

quiz_questions.question_text: Rusting of iron is a slow but spontaneous process. A student correctly concludes that:

options.text: Spontaneous does not mean fast; it only means DeltaG is negative for the process
Rusting is actually nonspontaneous because it happens so slowly over time
All spontaneous reactions must occur instantly without any delay whatsoever
Rusting is spontaneous only because it is exothermic and nothing else matters

quiz_questions.explanation: Spontaneity refers to thermodynamic favorability (DeltaG < 0), not speed. Rusting is spontaneous but slow because it has a high activation energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student dissolves sugar in hot tea. The sugar dissolves faster in hot tea than cold tea. In terms of thermodynamics, this is because:

options.text: Higher temperature increases TDeltaS, making DeltaG more negative and more favorable
Hot tea has a lower entropy than cold tea, which helps dissolve the sugar faster
Sugar has a higher specific heat than water, so it absorbs heat more readily
The enthalpy of dissolving becomes more negative at higher temperatures always

quiz_questions.explanation: Dissolving sugar increases entropy (DeltaS > 0). At higher T, the TDeltaS term is larger, making DeltaG more negative and the process more favorable.

quiz_questions.question_text: A power plant burns natural gas. The combustion is highly exothermic (DeltaH << 0) and produces gaseous CO2 and H2O (DeltaS > 0). This reaction is:

options.text: Spontaneous at all temperatures because both enthalpy and entropy favor it
Spontaneous only at low temperatures because the entropy increase is small
Nonspontaneous at all temperatures because combustion requires a spark first
At equilibrium at all temperatures because the gases balance the heat output

quiz_questions.explanation: Negative DeltaH and positive DeltaS both make DeltaG negative at all temperatures. The spark is activation energy, not a spontaneity issue.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is asked at what temperature a reaction with DeltaH = +40 kJ and DeltaS = +100 J/K becomes spontaneous. The answer is:

options.text: Above 400 K because T = DeltaH/DeltaS = 40,000 J / 100 J/K = 400 K
Below 400 K because lower temperatures favor endothermic reactions more
At exactly 0 K because all endothermic reactions are spontaneous at zero
At all temperatures because positive DeltaS always makes DeltaG negative

quiz_questions.explanation: At equilibrium, DeltaG = 0: T = DeltaH/DeltaS = 40,000/100 = 400 K. Above 400 K, TDeltaS > DeltaH, so DeltaG < 0 (spontaneous).
